## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**❓ "Two buyers race for the last unit — who wins, and can both win?"**
The mechanism: each checkout issues an atomic `DECRBY` on the Redis stock key; the operation is indivisible, so exactly one buyer sees a result ≥ 0 (wins) and the other sees < 0 and is rolled back with `INCRBY` and rejected — all before any payment runs.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "Only one person can take the last item off the shelf because the 'take one' action happens one at a time; the second person is told it's gone."
💬 **One-liner:** "An atomic decrement makes overselling impossible by construction — two people physically can't both get a non-negative count."

**❓ "What happens if the payment processor goes down mid-checkout?"**
The mechanism: payment is a saga step with an idempotency key; if the processor is unreachable we fail fast, run the compensation to release the inventory hold, and return "payment unavailable, no charge made" — no order is created and stock is restored.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "If the card system is down, we put the item back on the shelf and tell the user to try again — no money moves."
💬 **One-liner:** "Fail closed: release the hold, no charge, no order — correctness over completing the sale."

**❓ "What if Redis (the inventory counter) crashes during a flash sale?"**
The mechanism: Redis runs with AOF persistence and a replica that fails over; while it's unavailable we take the CP stance and refuse new reservations rather than risk overselling; on recovery we rebuild the counter from the Postgres inventory truth plus the open-reservation log.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "We'd rather briefly stop selling that item than accidentally sell more than we have."
💬 **One-liner:** "Postgres is the source of truth, so I rebuild Redis from it and never oversell while it's down."

**❓ "How do you handle a flash sale spiking one product to ~35K reads/s?"**
The mechanism: the PDP is served CDN-edge-first (~95% hit), the hot Redis key is replicated across nodes, and cache misses are coalesced with single-flight so only one request rebuilds on expiry; we pre-warm the cache before the drop.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "We serve the popular page from caches all over the world and let only one request rebuild it when it expires, so the database never gets hammered."
💬 **One-liner:** "Edge cache + key replication + single-flight turns 35K/s into a couple thousand DB reads."

**❓ "Can the same order be placed twice if the user double-clicks?"**
The mechanism: the client sends an idempotency key; the order service records it and a unique constraint on it means a repeated request returns the existing order instead of creating a new one or a new charge.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "A double-click reuses the same ticket number, so you get one order, not two."
💬 **One-liner:** "Idempotency key + unique constraint = exactly-once order from any number of retries."

**❓ "Can you oversell under any circumstance?"**
The mechanism: the only path that reduces stock is the atomic decrement, which can't go below zero without being undone; even a stale 'in stock' on the cached PDP just leads to a clean 'sold out' at checkout, never a confirmed oversell.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "The page might say in-stock a few seconds late, but the actual purchase is checked exactly, so we never sell more than we have."
💬 **One-liner:** "Display can be stale; the decrement is truth — oversell is structurally impossible."

**❓ "How do you stop a user from tampering with the price at checkout?"**
The mechanism: the order request carries no price — the server resolves the current SKU price from Postgres at checkout time using the userId derived from the JWT, so editing the request body does nothing.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "The price comes from our database, not from the user's request, so they can't change what they pay by editing the page."
💬 **One-liner:** "Server-side price resolution — the client never sends a price, so there's nothing to tamper with."

**❓ "What if someone forges another user's id to see their orders?"**
The mechanism: identity comes from the signed JWT, never a client-supplied id; every order read checks the order's userId against the token's subject, returning 403 on mismatch.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "We trust the login token, not whatever id the request claims, so you can only see your own orders."
💬 **One-liner:** "Authz off the JWT subject, not request params — forged ids get a 403."

**❓ "What's the most expensive part of this system?"**
The mechanism: CDN egress for images dominates — ~30TB/day billed per byte served — far exceeding database cost; the second is the memory-hungry Elasticsearch cluster.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "Sending all those product photos to browsers costs more than the databases do."
💬 **One-liner:** "It's CDN image egress, not the DB — so image optimization is the biggest cost lever."

**❓ "How would you cut costs without hurting the experience?"**
The mechanism: optimize images (modern formats, right-sizing, longer cache TTLs) to raise CDN hit ratio and shrink bytes; right-size the app/ES fleets with autoscaling tied to sale calendars; tier cold orders to cheap object storage.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "Make the images smaller and cached longer, and only run big servers when we're actually busy."
💬 **One-liner:** "Smaller cached images + autoscaling to the sale calendar = the biggest savings."

**❓ "How would you add product recommendations (follow-up #4)?"**
The mechanism: recommendations is a separate read-side service fed by the same Kafka event stream (views, purchases); it precomputes per-user/per-item recs offline and serves them from a cache, so it never touches the checkout path.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "We watch what people view and buy through the existing event stream and precompute suggestions, served from a fast cache."
💬 **One-liner:** "Recs is just another consumer of the event log + a precomputed cache — zero impact on the money path."

**❓ "How would you support the seller side (follow-up #1)?"**
The mechanism: add a seller service and a seller-scoped inventory model (stock per SKU per seller/warehouse), a listing-ingestion pipeline feeding the catalog + ES, and a payout ledger settled asynchronously off order events.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "Sellers get their own portal to list items and manage stock, and we pay them out based on completed orders."
💬 **One-liner:** "Multi-seller inventory + a listing pipeline + a payout ledger off the order stream."

**❓ "How would you make it multi-region for global low latency?"**
The mechanism: CDN already serves assets globally; add regional read replicas for catalog/search and keep a single write region for orders (or partition orders by region), with async cross-region replication and a warm standby for DR.
🗣️ **In plain words:** "Reads happen near the user everywhere; the actual purchase is written in one home region to keep the money logic simple."
💬 **One-liner:** "Global reads via replicas + CDN, single (or region-partitioned) write region for orders."

> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "It's an e-commerce portal — a giant browsable catalog welded to a small, sacred checkout pipeline. The design splits into two halves. The read half is the storefront: search runs on Elasticsearch kept fresh by a change stream, product pages are served CDN-edge-first and from a Redis cache, and carts live in DynamoDB — all tuned purely for latency and able to absorb a flash-sale spike of tens of thousands of reads a second on one product. The write half is the transaction core: the hardest problem is never overselling and never double-charging, and the mechanism that solves it is an atomic Redis inventory decrement that claims stock in a millisecond, followed by a payment saga with idempotency keys and compensations, committing the order to Postgres with RPO near zero. The two halves connect through a Kafka event stream that drives fulfilment, notifications, search indexing, and recommendations asynchronously. And every hard decision traces back to one principle: spend correctness on the money path, spend latency budget on the read path, and keep the two isolated so neither can take the other down."
